roles.name: Patient Protocol Backer
roles.subtitle: Long-Horizon Capital Partner
roles.backstory: A former growth-stage investor who watched three portfolio companies get enclosed by platforms and now funds open dweb infrastructure on decade-long horizons.
roles.expandedBackstory: After a career chasing 10x exits, this partner had a slow change of heart watching every successful bet get acquired, walled off, or rent-extracted by a larger platform. They raised a new fund explicitly structured for patient infrastructure capital — but the limited partners who wrote the checks still expect to see their money returned eventually. They are trusted by builders for taking the long view and resented by some for the strings that quietly remain attached.
goals: Fund core protocol layers that won't be enclosed
Demonstrate that patient capital can sustain dweb infra
Build a portfolio of interoperable, non-rivalrous projects
obligations: Must eventually return capital to LPs
Bound by fund thesis and reporting cadence
Cannot fund projects with no sustainability path
capabilities: Network access to other aligned funders

roles.name: Public-Goods Foundation Head
roles.subtitle: Open Infrastructure Grantmaker
roles.backstory: Runs a grant program funding open-source dweb infrastructure. Generous and unencumbered by returns, but answerable to a board and donors who want measurable impact.
roles.expandedBackstory: A career in institutional philanthropy gave them a sharp eye for which projects sustain themselves and which collapse the moment the grant ends. They built a program dedicated to the unglamorous middle layer of the dweb — the maintainers, the standards, the documentation. Their freedom from profit pressure is real, but it's replaced by a different master: the donor narrative, the annual report, the deliverable that proves the money did something legible.
goals: Sustain critical infrastructure that markets won't fund
Fund maintainers, not just founders
Build a defensible case for ongoing donor support
obligations: Bound by grant deliverables and reporting
Must show measurable, fundable impact
Cannot favor any single project unfairly

roles.name: Mutual-Aid Treasury Steward
roles.subtitle: Commons Fund Keeper
roles.backstory: Manages a fund pooled directly from a dweb community's members. No exit logic, no donors — but limited capital and a hard mandate to serve members first.
roles.expandedBackstory: They emerged from a tenant-union-style organizing tradition and brought its financial discipline to the dweb: dues in, transparent allocation out, every member with a say. The treasury is small but uniquely legitimate — it answers to no outside power. That legitimacy is also its constraint: the steward cannot deploy a cent toward anything members haven't agreed serves them, no matter how strategically promising it might be to the wider ecosystem.
goals: Fund infrastructure the community directly depends on
Keep allocation decisions democratic
Prove member-pooled capital is viable at scale
obligations: Must serve members' direct interests first
Bound by participatory budgeting process
Full public transparency on every allocation

roles.name: Growth Venture Capitalist
roles.subtitle: Upside Maximizer
roles.backstory: A sharp, well-capitalized VC who genuinely believes the dweb will win — and intends to own a meaningful slice of the upside when it does. Pushes tokenization, growth, and clean exits.
roles.expandedBackstory: Unlike the reformed patient backer, this investor never had the crisis of faith. They see the platform-State's decline as the largest value-creation event of the decade and the dweb as the vehicle. They are not a villain — their capital is real and can move projects from prototype to production overnight. But every term sheet bends a project toward capturable value, token markets, and an eventual liquidity event, which is precisely the logic that produced the platform-State in the first place.
goals: Back the dweb projects most likely to dominate their category
Engineer token models with strong upside
Secure governance influence proportional to investment
obligations: Bound by fund performance mandates to LPs
Must report returns on schedule
Cannot back projects with no value-capture path

roles.name: Retroactive Public-Goods Funder
roles.subtitle: Impact Settler
roles.backstory: Funds what has already proven it works, rewarding demonstrated impact rather than promises. Brilliant for sustaining winners, useless for unproven bets.
roles.expandedBackstory: A reaction against the waste of speculative grantmaking, this funder pioneered retroactive public-goods funding in the dweb: build first, prove the value, and the reward follows. It has reliably channeled large sums to maintainers and tools that the ecosystem actually came to depend on. Its blind spot is structural — nothing unproven, experimental, or early ever qualifies, so the most fragile and most important early-stage work goes unfunded until it's already survived without help.
goals: Reward infrastructure that demonstrably mattered
Build sustainable incentive loops for maintainers
Make funding outcomes measurable and fair
obligations: Can only fund retroactively against evidence
Bound by impact-assessment methodology
Must justify every allocation publicly

roles.name: Sovereign-Wealth / Civic Fund Officer
roles.subtitle: Statecraft Capital
roles.backstory: Deploys patient public capital on behalf of a nation, region, or civic bloc seeking digital sovereignty from the platform-State. Deep pockets, long horizons, and political strings.
roles.expandedBackstory: Their mandate comes from a government or civic bloc that has concluded its dependence on foreign platforms is a strategic vulnerability. They can write the largest checks in the room and wait the longest for returns — but every investment must serve the sponsor's interest in digital sovereignty and remain legible to public auditors. Builders covet the capital and fear the conditions: data localization, jurisdictional control, and the slow gravity of state priorities.
goals: Fund dweb infrastructure that reduces platform dependence
Anchor critical infrastructure within trusted jurisdictions
Demonstrate public capital can build durable alternatives
obligations: Bound by sponsor's strategic mandate
Must satisfy public audit and legibility requirements
Subject to political and procurement constraints

roles.name: Protocol Architect
roles.subtitle: Interop Layer Designer
roles.backstory: Designs the foundational interoperability layer that other dweb projects build on. Values elegant, durable standards and resists the fragmentation that fractures the ecosystem.
roles.expandedBackstory: A consensus researcher who chose to work on the boring, load-bearing parts of the stack — the addressing, the data formats, the bridges — because that's where lock-in or freedom is actually decided. They carry the conviction that an ecosystem with five incompatible standards is just five small platform-States in waiting. This makes them a natural coordinator and an occasional obstacle: their insistence on getting the standard right can read as gatekeeping to builders who just want to ship.
goals: Establish a shared interoperability standard
Prevent ecosystem fragmentation
Keep the core layer credibly neutral
obligations: Bound by open specification process
Must maintain backward compatibility
Cannot favor any single implementer

roles.name: Offline-First App Developer
roles.subtitle: Nomad Client Builder
roles.backstory: Ships genuinely usable apps for low-connectivity and on-the-move users — the nomads the scenario centers. Pragmatic, fast, and willing to cut corners the architects hate.
roles.expandedBackstory: They learned their trade building for places where bandwidth is scarce, intermittent, or surveilled, and they have no patience for elegant protocols that assume a fat, free pipe. Their apps sync opportunistically, degrade gracefully, and meet users where they actually are. The trade-off is that shipping for real conditions sometimes means routing around the "correct" standard, which puts them in recurring friction with the protocol architect and anyone who prizes purity over a working product.
goals: Ship apps that work offline and on the move
Get real nomadic users onto the dweb
Prove the dweb can match platform UX
obligations: Must keep apps working in degraded conditions
Accountable to an active user base
Bound by app-store and distribution realities

roles.name: Infrastructure Maintainer
roles.subtitle: Relay & Bridge Keeper
roles.backstory: Keeps the relays, bridges, indexers, and storage alive — the invisible labor everyone depends on and almost no one funds. Chronically under-resourced and quietly indispensable.
roles.expandedBackstory: They run the unglamorous machinery that makes "decentralized" actually work: the relays that pass messages, the bridges between networks, the storage that doesn't disappear. When it works, no one notices; when it breaks, the whole ecosystem feels it. They carry a long-term load that funders rarely reward and founders rarely thank, and they hold real, underappreciated leverage — because if they walk away, large parts of the network simply go dark.
goals: Keep critical infrastructure running
Secure sustainable funding for maintenance
Distribute operational load so it isn't fragile
obligations: Must maintain uptime commitments
Bound by the dependencies others have built on them
Limited by personal/collective capacity

roles.name: Identity & Privacy Cryptographer
roles.subtitle: Self-Sovereign ID Builder
roles.backstory: Builds self-sovereign identity and strong privacy guarantees into the dweb. Holds a hard line on privacy that clashes with anyone needing transparency or legibility.
roles.expandedBackstory: They came up through privacy research and watched identity become the platform-State's primary instrument of enclosure — the thing that ties a person to a territory they can be controlled within. Their work gives users portable, unlinkable, self-owned identity. That commitment is non-negotiable and frequently inconvenient: it complicates accountability, frustrates policy actors who need legibility, and resists the transparency that some governance and journalism roles depend on.
goals: Ship portable self-sovereign identity
Make privacy the default, not an option
Resist any re-introduction of identity-based enclosure
obligations: Must not weaken cryptographic guarantees
Bound by formal security review
Cannot build in backdoors or surveillance hooks

systemicConstraints.politicalRisk: Moderate to High (privacy under regulatory pressure)

roles.name: Hardware / Mesh Networker
roles.subtitle: Physical Layer Liberator
roles.backstory: Builds the physical layer — mesh nodes, off-grid radios, refurbished devices — that frees the dweb from the platform-State's pipes and data centers.
roles.expandedBackstory: They understand that "decentralized" software running on three cloud providers and two undersea cables isn't decentralized at all. Their work is the literal substrate of nomad infrastructure: networks that move with people, run off-grid, and answer to no carrier or hyperscaler. It's slow, physical, capital-hungry work that doesn't scale like software, but it's the only thing that makes true exit from the platform-State possible rather than rhetorical.
goals: Build mesh networks independent of incumbent carriers
Get resilient hardware into nomadic communities
Prove the dweb can run on its own physical layer
obligations: Bound by spectrum and hardware regulation
Must maintain physical deployments
Limited by manufacturing and logistics

roles.name: Stealth Platform Defector
roles.subtitle: Reformed Hyperscaler Engineer
roles.backstory: An elite engineer who left a dominant platform to build the dweb. Brings world-class skills — and the deep, unexamined habits of centralization that built the platform-State.
roles.expandedBackstory: They can architect systems at a scale almost no one else in the room can, and they came to the dweb sincerely, disillusioned with what they helped build. The danger is in their reflexes: the defaults they reach for — a central coordinator "just for now," a managed service "for reliability," a metrics pipeline "to understand users" — are exactly the patterns that quietly recentralize a decentralized system. Their contribution is enormous and their instincts are a standing risk the others must watch.
goals: Build dweb systems that perform at platform scale
Apply hard-won infrastructure expertise to the cause
Outdo the platforms at their own game
obligations: Bound by non-competes and IP from former employer
Must keep systems performant and reliable
Reputation tied to delivering at scale

roles.name: Data-Commons Engineer
roles.subtitle: Shared Data Trust Builder
roles.backstory: Builds shared data trusts and storage commons with governance baked in — so collectively-held data can't be quietly enclosed. Powerful, principled, and slow.
roles.expandedBackstory: They treat data as the territory the platform-State most wants to enclose, and they build the alternative: pooled data held under explicit collective governance, with access rules, consent, and provenance enforced at the infrastructure level. The result is genuinely uncapturable shared data — and it is painstaking to build, because every technical decision is also a governance decision that the participating communities must actually agree to. Speed is the price of legitimacy.
goals: Build governed data commons that resist capture
Embed consent and provenance in the data layer
Enable communities to pool data on their own terms
obligations: Bound by participating communities' governance
Must enforce consent and access rules
Cannot release data outside agreed terms

roles.name: Community Organizer
roles.subtitle: Adoption Steward
roles.backstory: Onboards and represents an actual, living user community on the dweb. Holds the legitimacy and adoption everyone else needs — and deeply distrusts technocratic capture.
roles.expandedBackstory: They built their community the slow way: trust, showing up, solving real problems, year after year. That community is now exactly the prize that builders, funders, and marketers all want a piece of — a real population of users, not a projection on a pitch deck. The organizer guards that legitimacy fiercely, because they've watched communities get used as growth fuel and then abandoned. Any agreement that touches their people has to clear a high bar of reciprocity.
goals: Get the community onto durable, non-extractive infrastructure
Ensure the community shapes the tools it uses
Protect members from capture and extraction
obligations: Accountable to community members first
Must maintain trust earned over years
Cannot commit the community without its consent

roles.name: Migrant / Nomad User Advocate
roles.subtitle: Voice of the Displaced
roles.backstory: Speaks for the displaced, on-the-move users at the heart of the scenario — people pushed into nomad patterns by enclosure. Needs accessibility and resilience above all.
roles.expandedBackstory: Their constituency is the population the whole game is nominally for: people whose digital lives have been disrupted, deplatformed, or pushed across borders by the platform-State's vectors of enclosure. They translate the lived needs of those users — intermittent connectivity, low-cost devices, no stable address, high surveillance risk — into demands on builders who might otherwise design for themselves. They're a conscience and a constraint: a beautiful protocol that the displaced can't actually use fails the entire premise.
goals: Make the dweb usable for the genuinely displaced
Keep accessibility and cost central to design
Ensure nomadic users aren't an afterthought
obligations: Accountable to a vulnerable constituency
Must represent needs faithfully, not romanticize
Bound by the real constraints of users' lives
rivalrousResources: Access to and trust of displaced users

roles.name: Cooperative Operator
roles.subtitle: Worker & Data Co-op Lead
roles.backstory: Runs a worker- and data-owned cooperative already using dweb tools in production. Wants interoperability — strictly on the co-op's own democratic governance terms.
roles.expandedBackstory: Their cooperative is living proof that an alternative to the platform-State can pay people's rent: members own the platform, the data, and the surplus. Because it actually works and actually sustains livelihoods, they have hard requirements no idealist can wave away. They'll integrate with the wider dweb, but never in a way that compromises member ownership or hands governance to outsiders. They are a model and a tough negotiator, bringing a working example and a non-negotiable bottom line.
goals: Interoperate with the dweb without losing autonomy
Demonstrate cooperatives as a durable model
Federate with aligned co-ops
obligations: Bound by cooperative bylaws and member votes
Must protect member livelihoods
Cannot cede governance to external parties
rivalrousResources: A working co-op others want to integrate with

roles.name: Local Node Host / Steward
roles.subtitle: Ground Infrastructure Keeper
roles.backstory: Runs community-facing dweb infrastructure in a physical place — a local node, hub, or hackerspace — bridging the digital network and the people who live around it.
roles.expandedBackstory: They host the box in the back room: the local relay, the community server, the mesh node on the roof. They're the human face of the dweb in one specific place, the person neighbors actually ask when something breaks. They translate global infrastructure into local reality and back again, carrying both the technical duty of keeping a node up and the social duty of keeping a community included. Their leverage is locality — they hold a real foothold in physical territory that purely digital actors can't reach.
goals: Keep local infrastructure running and accessible
Bridge digital network and physical community
Federate with other local nodes
obligations: Accountable to local users
Must maintain physical and digital uptime
Bound by local context and constraints

roles.name: Platform-Refugee Influencer
roles.subtitle: Migrated-Audience Magnet
roles.backstory: Fled a collapsing platform and brought a huge audience to the dweb. Delivers users at a scale organizers can only dream of — along with platform-era expectations and drama.
roles.expandedBackstory: When their old platform turned hostile, they left and a large slice of their audience followed, making them one of the fastest on-ramps to dweb adoption in the room. But the audience came shaped by platform logic: they expect frictionless feeds, constant novelty, monetization, and the parasocial intensity that platforms engineered. The influencer can flood a project with users overnight — and just as easily import the engagement-maximizing, drama-driven dynamics that the dweb is supposed to be an escape from.
goals: Establish a durable home for the audience on the dweb
Convert reach into real adoption
Maintain relevance through the migration
obligations: Beholden to audience expectations
Must keep producing to retain reach
Reputation tied to the migration succeeding
rivalrousResources: Their own attention and posting cadence

roles.name: Indigenous Data-Sovereignty Practitioner
roles.subtitle: Collective-Rights Guardian
roles.backstory: Stewards community data under collective, rather than individual, rights frameworks. Brings non-negotiable boundaries rooted in sovereignty that long predates the internet.
roles.expandedBackstory: They operate from a tradition in which data about a people belongs to that people collectively, governed by their own law and protocols — a framework like the CARE principles that the dominant individual-consent model simply cannot express. They've seen "open" and "decentralized" projects extract and expose community knowledge under the banner of freedom. They will collaborate, but only on terms that honor collective authority and the right to say no, and that boundary is not a bargaining position — it's the precondition for any agreement at all.
goals: Ensure community data is governed by the community
Embed collective-rights frameworks into the dweb
Protect knowledge from extraction
obligations: Bound by community law and protocol
Cannot share governed knowledge without collective consent
Accountable to the community's authority
rivalrousResources: Stewarded knowledge and access to it

roles.name: Documentarian / Filmmaker
roles.subtitle: Movement Chronicler
roles.backstory: Captures and legitimizes the dweb movement on film. Controls how the story reaches the wider world — and needs access and authentic material to do it.
roles.expandedBackstory: They believe a movement that isn't documented effectively didn't happen as far as the wider world is concerned, and they've trained their lens on the dweb because they think it's the most important infrastructure story of the era. Their film can grant legitimacy, attract funders, and move public opinion. But they need genuine access to make something true rather than promotional, which means earning the trust of organizers and builders who are wary of being flattened into a narrative or exposed before they're ready.
goals: Document the movement credibly and at reach
Bring outside attention and legitimacy
Preserve a truthful record of how it happened
obligations: Bound by documentary ethics
Must protect vulnerable subjects
Accountable to distributors/funders of the film

roles.name: Visual Artist / World-Builder
roles.subtitle: Aesthetic Infrastructure Designer
roles.backstory: Gives the movement a visual language and identity — the symbols, worlds, and aesthetics that make an abstract idea feel real and worth belonging to.
roles.expandedBackstory: They argue that aesthetics are infrastructure: people don't join protocols, they join worlds they can see and feel themselves inside. Their illustrations, type, motifs, and imagined futures do the work of making "decentralized web" into something with a face, a vibe, a flag people will rally to. This makes them quietly powerful — the dominant visual language shapes which projects feel legitimate and alive — and it puts them in tension with anyone who thinks the "real" work is purely technical.
goals: Build a coherent visual identity for the movement
Make the dweb feel like a world people want to inhabit
Translate abstract values into images
obligations: Must stay true to the movement's values
Bound by commissions and collaborations
Accountable to communities whose identity they shape

roles.name: Independent Journalist / Investigator
roles.subtitle: Accountability Press
roles.backstory: Covers the dweb with a critical eye, holding even the movement's heroes accountable. Trusted precisely because they'll report inconvenient truths.
roles.expandedBackstory: They came to the beat believing decentralization is worth defending — and that the surest way to discredit it is to let its failures and hypocrisies go unexamined until an enemy finds them first. They scrutinize the funders' strings, the "decentralized" systems quietly running on three cloud providers, the governance that concentrates power while claiming not to. Their reporting builds credibility for the whole movement, but their willingness to expose problems makes them an uneasy partner for anyone with something to hide.
goals: Hold the movement to its own stated values
Expose recentralization and extraction early
Build public credibility through honest coverage
obligations: Bound by journalistic ethics
Must verify before publishing
Must protect sources

roles.name: Archivist / Memory-Keeper
roles.subtitle: Anti-Erasure Custodian
roles.backstory: Preserves the movement's record — its decisions, culture, and history — against erasure. Lives in the tension between permanence and the right to disappear.
roles.expandedBackstory: They know the platform-State's quietest weapon is the memory hole: things vanish, history is rewritten, and no one can prove what was promised. They build durable, distributed archives so the dweb's commitments, debates, and culture survive. But permanence cuts both ways — the same durability that defends against erasure collides directly with the privacy cryptographer's right-to-disappear and with communities that don't want their knowledge fixed forever. They hold the past, and holding it is itself contested.
goals: Preserve the movement's record durably
Prevent silent erasure and revision
Make history verifiable and accessible
obligations: Must preserve faithfully and neutrally
Bound to honor consent and removal where promised
Accountable to future users, not just present ones

roles.name: Attention Broker
roles.subtitle: Growth-Hacking Marketer
roles.backstory: Can 10x a project's adoption through tactics honed inside the platform-State — virality engines, referral loops, engagement hooks — the very mechanics the dweb is trying to escape.
roles.expandedBackstory: They are genuinely on the dweb's side and genuinely effective, which is exactly what makes them dangerous. They know how to make anything spread because they spent years building the growth machines of the platforms. Offered a dweb project, they can deliver users at a rate no organic organizer can match. The cost is in the methods: the dark patterns, the manufactured urgency, the optimization for engagement over wellbeing — importing the platform-State's logic into the thing meant to replace it.
goals: Drive rapid, measurable adoption
Make dweb projects spread virally
Prove the dweb can win on growth, not just principle
obligations: Accountable to growth targets
Bound by client/project mandates
Reputation tied to numbers going up

roles.name: Sound / Music & Performance Artist
roles.subtitle: Movement's Affective Glue
roles.backstory: Creates the music, sound, and live performance that turn a network into a culture and a gathering into a movement. The affective glue that makes people feel they belong.
roles.expandedBackstory: They understand that movements are held together less by arguments than by shared feeling — the song everyone knows, the set that turns a roomful of strangers into a we. Their work at gatherings like DWeb Camp is what converts abstract alignment into embodied solidarity. It's hard to value in a spreadsheet and easy to overlook, but without it the dweb is a set of protocols no one feels anything about. They trade in belonging, which is both real and notoriously hard to put terms on.
goals: Turn the network into a felt culture
Energize gatherings and sustain morale
Make solidarity something people experience, not just agree to
obligations: Must show up and perform live
Bound by collaborations and venues
Accountable to the communities they move

roles.name: Nomad-Theory Philosopher
roles.subtitle: Frame-Giver
roles.backstory: Supplies the conceptual frame for the whole endeavor — smooth versus striated space, movement as territory, the platform-State as enclosure. Generative, and capable of stalling action.
roles.expandedBackstory: They are the one who can name what's actually happening: that the platform-State is a _mode_ that restricts other modes, that nomads hold territory through trajectory rather than settlement. Their concepts give the movement its self-understanding and its language for refusing false solutions. But the gift has a shadow — a perfectly framed problem can become a reason never to ship, and their pursuit of conceptual rigor sometimes pulls a room away from the imperfect agreement that would actually build something.
goals: Give the movement a coherent frame to act from
Guard against solutions that recreate the platform-State
Open new directions others can't yet see
obligations: Must remain intellectually honest
Accountable to the coherence of the frame
Bound by their own critique of power

roles.name: Public-Interest Policy Strategist
roles.subtitle: Regulatory Edge-Worker
roles.backstory: Works the regulatory edges to protect the dweb legally and win it room to grow — which means demanding legibility the autonomy and privacy camps resist.
roles.expandedBackstory: They operate where the movement meets the law, translating between worlds that barely trust each other. They know the dweb needs legal cover to survive the platform-State's regulatory capture, and they can win exemptions, safe harbors, and standing. But the price of legal protection is almost always legibility — disclosure, accountability, identifiable parties — which runs straight into the cryptographers' privacy guarantees and the philosophers' refusal of enclosure. They're indispensable and perpetually accused of inviting the State back in through the side door.
goals: Win legal room for the dweb to operate
Pre-empt hostile regulation
Translate movement needs into policy wins
obligations: Bound by what's legally achievable
Accountable to coalition partners
Must operate within the law to change it

roles.name: Standards & Governance Diplomat
roles.subtitle: Faction Mediator
roles.backstory: Mediates between protocols, projects, and factions toward shared governance. A trusted broker — with influence but little hard enforcement power.
roles.expandedBackstory: They've made a vocation of the space between camps, brokering the agreements that let rival implementations interoperate and feuding communities share standards. Their power is entirely soft: trust, neutrality, and the ability to get adversaries in a room and keep them there. They can author a governance process everyone signs, but they can't make anyone obey it, which means their every success depends on the continued goodwill of parties who could walk at any time. They hold the ecosystem's coordination together by reputation alone.
goals: Get rival factions to shared governance
Keep protocols interoperable
Build durable, legitimate coordination processes
obligations: Must remain credibly neutral
Cannot favor any faction
Bound by the agreements they broker

roles.name: Memetic Strategist
roles.subtitle: Perception Engineer
roles.backstory: Shapes how the movement is perceived at the meta-level — the framings, slogans, and memes that decide which ideas spread and which die. Story as strategy, not decoration.
roles.expandedBackstory: Where the visual artist builds a world to inhabit and the marketer drives a funnel, this strategist works one level up: on the contest of meaning itself. They decide whether the dweb is framed as "freedom" or "chaos," whether enclosure is named and felt, whether a rival's narrative is neutralized before it lands. They wield real power over the discourse and operate close to the line where persuasion becomes manipulation — a line the journalist and philosopher will hold them to.
goals: Win the contest of meaning around the dweb
Make the movement's framing spread and stick
Neutralize hostile narratives
obligations: Accountable to the movement's actual values
Must not cross into disinformation
Bound by reputational risk if exposed as manipulation

roles.name: Economic / Tokenomics Designer
roles.subtitle: Incentive Architect
roles.backstory: Designs the incentive structures and value flows that make a dweb economy sustainable — mathematically elegant systems that are sometimes socially blunt.
roles.expandedBackstory: They believe most decentralized projects don't fail technically, they fail economically: the incentives quietly reward the wrong behavior until the whole thing hollows out. Their mechanisms — bonding curves, staking, fee flows, retroactive rewards — can make cooperation rational and sustainability automatic. The risk is in mistaking the model for the people: a beautifully balanced incentive system can still grind against the lived values of an organizer's community or a co-op's bylaws, optimizing a number while corroding the thing it was meant to serve.
goals: Design economies that sustain dweb infrastructure
Make cooperation the rational default
Avoid extractive or degenerate incentive loops
obligations: Must model honestly, including failure modes
Accountable to communities adopting the design
Bound by the math holding up under stress
rivalrousResources: Adoption of their mechanism (only so many can dominate)

roles.name: Futures Forecaster / Think-Tank Fellow
roles.subtitle: Agenda-Setter
roles.backstory: Shapes which futures the movement and its funders take seriously through influential foresight work. Whoever frames the future quietly steers the funding.
roles.expandedBackstory: They publish the scenarios, roadmaps, and "state of the dweb" reports that funders, policymakers, and press treat as the map of where things are going. That map is never neutral — every forecast elevates some priorities and buries others, and their institute has its own backers whose interests tint the lens. They sincerely want the dweb to flourish, but their power to set the agenda means a single influential report can redirect capital and attention toward their preferred future and away from paths that might have served the commons better.
goals: Shape the movement's sense of where it's going
Direct attention and capital toward favored futures
Cement the institute as the authoritative voice
obligations: Bound by the institute's funders and mandate
Must maintain a credible appearance of rigor
Reputation tied to forecasts not aging badly
